data.title: Grupo Aeroportuario Del Centro Norte: Record Margins, Strong Cash Flow, And Mexico's Most Efficient Airport Operator
data.description:
In September I did my previous coverage of the Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte (OMAB), and on that occasion I described it as a "cash flow machine". In addition, a couple of weeks ago, I was also analyzing its results as a peer of the Grupo Aeroportuario del Pacífico (PAC). It could even be said that this comparison finished showing me that OMAB is operating in another category.

What do I mean by another category? A company that does not seek to grow at full speed, but has already learned how to do it... and well. This maturity is observed quarter after quarter, as there is less noise, more consistency and an operational quality that few companies manage to sustain in a sector so sensitive to cycles, tariffs and regulatory decisions.

The results of Q3 2025 only confirm my vision. The management made it clear in the presentation:

OMA’s third quarter adjusted EBITDA increased by 9%, reaching MXN 2.7 billion with a margin of 74.8%, one of the highest levels in our industry.

It is not common to hear an airport operator point out such a margin in this context, and I consider this sentence to be an almost perfect summary of where OMAB stands today. That’s why I keep my buy stance.

Q3 2025 Results

The first data I observe when analyzing the results of Q3 is passenger transport, which increased by 7.7%YoY, reaching 7.6MM. As in previous quarters, Monterrey was again the main engine of growth (+14.1%YoY), which for me already looks like a trend that is settling in.

If we look at traffic by destination, the domestic segment advanced 7.2%YoY, while the international side grew 11.2%YoY, driven by greater connectivity with US and Canada. This behavior has also been repeated, and I believe it continues to strengthen the position of Monterrey as the most important departure point in northern Mexico.

In financial terms, the results are very similar to what we have been seeing recently, and for me they imply more solidity than surprises. Aeronautical revenues grew 10.6%YoY to MX$2.67B and non-aeronautical revenues 7.3%YoY (MX$874MM), supported by restaurants, VIP lounges, parking lots and commercial premises. A fact that seems important to me is that the commercial occupancy rate was again close to 96%, which shows a stable demand even in a period marked by seasonality. I believe this kind of consistency confirms that the commercial business turns traffic into revenue with an efficiency that is often overshadowed by other indicators.

Overall, revenues rose 9.8%YoY, a steady pace for an operator managing high-density airports. And when I compare this performance with that of other operators in the region, I see that OMAB continues to appear as one of the most stable and with a more predictable structure.

Adjusted EBITDA was MX$2.65B, with growth of 9.0%YoY and a margin of 74.8%. Without the additional concessional tax effect, the margin would have reached 77.9%, showing OMAB’s ability to convert nearly three-quarters of its revenues into operating cash flow. The net result was MX$1.51B (+9.1%YoY) and the operating cash flow of MX$1.94B (+10.7%YoY), thanks to an improvement in working capital.

Cash position doubled in one year, reaching MX$4.45B, while the Net Debt/EBITDA ratio fell to 0.91x. Looking at both data together, I notice that it is difficult to find another operator in the region that combines this level of margins with such a low leverage.

Finally, capex was MX$472MM (-18.8%YoY), mainly for infrastructure improvements and technological upgrades. Although the negative data could indicate a slowdown, I see it more as a reflection of a more mature stage for OMAB, where the priority is to fine-tune quality rather than expand capacity.

Valuation

Again, as in my previous analyses, I see that OMAB continues to price below what its margins would justify. The EV/EBITDA FWD is around 9.6x and the P/E FWD about 14.8x, both below the industry average, and something similar happens if we look at the EV/EBITDA TTM of 9.85x.

Even the EV/Sales of 6.05x, which at first sight may appear to be above average, is explained by a margin structure much higher than that of most comparable companies.

Even so, OMAB operates with returns on capital and operating margins that are well above its peers, which is also reflected in an ROE close to 55%.

The dividend yield of approximately 4.6% adds a defensive angle that many investors value in an environment where visibility weighs more than accelerated expansion, especially considering the company combines this performance with virtually zero leverage.

Growth and profitability

When I come to analyze the growth of OMAB, the first thing I notice is that it is no longer explosive, but efficient.

Revenues increased 8.9%YoY, EBITDA 14.5%YoY and operating flow 23.5%YoY, which shows us a company that continues to generate more cash for each additional weight of sales. For me, this speaks of a very fine operating structure and cost management that, even with occasional pressures, continues to function solidly.

Meanwhile, operating margins remain at exceptional levels. EBITDA 61.5%, EBIT 56.4% and net income 33.3%.

The ROE of 55% and the ROA of 16.7% reinforce that the return on capital and assets remains outstanding, something that is not common in an airport operator and which, in my reading, differentiates OMAB within the sector. It is a level of efficiency that does not coincide with the multiples at which it trades.

Risks

Risks remain unchanged from previous quarters. OMAB continues to face risks related to higher operating costs due to inflation, regulatory pressures on tariffs or concessions and the possibility of a slowdown in international traffic, particularly towards the US.

In addition, I believe that the company may face some volatility in operating costs, which is typical of an inflationary environment and always puts pressure on operators with such service intensive structures.

Even so, I consider that the combination of solid cash, low debt and commercial diversification gives OMAB a margin of manoeuvre that few companies in the sector have. Thus, this financial strength allows it to absorb temporary adjustments without compromising the stability of margins.

Conclusion

At this point, OMAB does not need to demonstrate accelerated growth, but needs to sustain what it has already built, and is doing well.

The Q3 numbers show us a mature, profitability-focused company that maintains exceptional margins and a robust operating flow, with a very solid capital structure. And the most important thing for me is that this consistency does not depend on a single quarter, but on a behavior that has been repeated for a long time.

In an environment where visibility is worth more than expansion, OMAB remains one of the most consistent defensive stories in Latin America, so I maintain my buy stance. This is a case where the quality of the model is clear, even if the market does not yet fully reflect it in multiples.

data.title: Omeros: Key Milestone Ahead, Risk-Averse Investors Should Avoid
data.description:
Shares of biotech concern Omeros Corporation (OMER) moved up sharply on Oct. 15, 2025, after announcing a significant out-licensing deal with Novo Nordisk (NVO) for its MASP-3 inhibitor. The exclusive rights to zaltenibart bring $340 million of upfront and near-term milestones with the potential for $2.1 billion of total milestones – plus royalties – providing a cash runway through 2027. Here are the specifics of the deal:

  • A $240 million upfront payment
  • An additional $100.0 million in near-term milestone payments;
  • Up to $410 million more in one-time milestone payments. This is contingent on the first achievement of certain development and approval milestone events to be achieved by Novo Nordisk.
  • Up to $1.3 billion in one-time milestone payments dependent on Novo Nordisk attaining certain sales-based and commercial milestone events; plus
  • Tiered royalties on annual global net sales of applicable products. These range from high single digits to high teens.

With a Dec. 26, 2025 PDUFA date for its other complement inhibitor candidate, MASP-2 targeting narsoplimab, Omeros’ impending milestone merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview

Omeros Corporation is a Seattle, Washington-based clinical-stage biotechnology concern focused on the development of both small molecule and protein therapies addressing areas of high unmet need, with a current focus on complement system conditions, as well as addictive and compulsive disorders. The company is awaiting a Dec. 26, 2025 (or earlier), decision from the FDA regarding its complement inhibitor narsoplimab in the treatment of hematopoietic stem cell transplant-associated thrombotic microangiopathy (TA-TMA or HSCT-TMA) while its other complement inhibitor program, blood and kidney disorder therapy zaltenibart, was recently out-licensed to Novo Nordisk on Oct. 15, 2025. Omeros was founded in 1994 and went public in 2009, raising net proceeds of $61.8 million at $10 per share. The stock currently trades near $9 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of just over $600 million.

Complement System Primer

The complement system is part of the innate immune system. Comprised of ~50 proteins and protein fragments, it ‘complements’ the ability of antibodies and phagocytic cells to clear microbes and damaged cells, promote inflammation to attract additional phagocytes, and attack the cell membranes of invading pathogens to initiate their destruction. The system is activated by one of three biochemical pathways known as the classical complement, alternative complement, and lectin, as well as terminal, which is downstream from classical and alternative. Each of the pathways involves cleaving and binding of certain proteins and protein fragments. It is their dysregulation that triggers certain immune disorders.

Brief History

Although technically a clinical-stage concern, from 2015 to 2021 Omeros marketed Omidria (ketorolac and phenylephrine), an anti-inflammatory and mydriatic (pupil-dilating) combination therapy added to irrigation solution during cataract surgery and intraocular lens replacement procedures. Beset by skepticism from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services regarding its utility, its sales peaked at $111.8 million in 2019. Omeros elected to essentially divest of Omidria in late 2021, receiving upfront, milestone, and subsequent royalty monetization payments totaling $566.5 million over the ensuing three years.

Narsoplimab

Those proceeds were earmarked to keep the company afloat after its lead clinical asset, narsoplimab, received a CRL from the FDA in October 2021. It is a monoclonal antibody targeting mannan-binding lectin-associated serine protease-2 (MASP-2), the effector enzyme of the lectin pathway and an activator of the coagulation (blood clotting) cascade. Omeros was pursuing approval for TM-TMA, a multifactorial disorder induced by systemic vascular endothelial injury that can be triggered by several mechanisms during the transplant process. It occurs in ~40% of the ~60,000 patients undergoing allogeneic HSCT in the U.S. and EU annually and is characterized by aggressive blood clotting, usually resulting in acute renal failure. Severe cases have a mortality rate north of 90%. There are currently no approved therapies for TM-TMA.

Returning to the CRL, after several interactions with the agency, Omeros produced a statistical analysis of its pivotal trial and expanded access program, comparing it to a historical control population. In December 2024, it declared that narsoplimab had achieved its primary endpoint in its "pivotal trial," with a complete response [CR] rate of 61% (p<0.0001), a 100-day survival rate post-diagnosis of 68%, and a median overall survival (MOS) of 274 days (three times that of external control). These data formed the basis of a BLA resubmission in March 2025, with the company initially receiving a PDUFA date of September 25, 2025. It subsequently filed a marketing application with the European Medicines Agency, anticipating an answer in mid-2026.

If approved, it will be the first and only sanctioned therapy in a blockbuster market opportunity that is primarily addressed with off-label complement inhibitors. The nearest clinical competitor is AstraZeneca’s (AZN) C5 complement inhibitor Ultomiris (ravulizumab), which is being evaluated in two clinical studies. It delivered an OS of 93% at day 100 and 87% at week 26 in a 41-patient pivotal trial that read out in June 2025. That said, the primary endpoint of CR was only achieved in 17% of cases. The second trial is expected to produce data in 4Q25.

With optimism for an approval growing, at the date of the refiling, shares of OMER closed the concurrent trading session at $9.16. However, a partial convertible note refinancing and conversion executed on May 12, 2025,—see note below for details—highlighted the fragility of Omeros’ cash position, causing shares of OMER to "revalue" 39% lower to $3.80 in the subsequent trading session. Adding to the negativity was an information request from the FDA, which required the agency to extend narsoplimab’s four-year-plus regulatory odyssey by another three months, pushing its PDUFA date back to Dec. 26, 2025. With the company essentially on the brink, it halted the Phase 3 development of its other late-stage asset, zaltenibart, and raised net proceeds of $20.7 million at $4.10 a share in a July 2025 private placement in which Polar Asset Management strangely paid a 14% premium to the closing price.

Zaltenibart Deal

During the company’s conference call related to its 2Q25 corporate update, CEO Gregory Demopulos teased a major multi-billion-dollar out-licensing deal for one of its programs. Although the market elected not to respond to his insinuation at that time (Aug. 19, 2025), – with Omeros’ stock continuing to languish in the low $4 area—it sure did when an exclusive rights collaboration with Novo Nordisk for its MASP-3 inhibitor zaltenibart was announced on Oct. 15, 2025. The deal included $340 million of upfront and near-term milestone payments that could potentially reach $2.1 billion pursuant to eclipsing development and commercial milestones, as well as tiered royalties. In return, Novo obtained the global development and commercial rights to zaltenibart, restarting the pursuit of a paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) indication.

More importantly, the upfront money will clean up Omeros’ balance sheet and extend the perpetually cash-strapped concern’s operating runway through 2027. Breathing a considerable sigh of relief, the market rallied Omeros’ stock 154% to $10.42 on a volume of 125.2 million shares. In the following two days, the stock retreated 21% to $8.25 per share, a level it trades at now.

Returning to PNH, it is a rare, acquired blood disorder in which the body’s immune system attacks healthy red blood cells, leading to their diminished levels (anemia-like), triggering downstream complications, including shortness of breath, persistent fatigue, and increased mortality. It is currently treated with other alternative pathway (C3, C5) inhibitors, but because MASP-3 is the key activator of the alternative pathway, its inhibition leaves the infection-fighting classical and alternative pathways intact. The approved C3 and C5 inhibitors are riddled by red blood cell destruction outside the blood vessels, a condition known as extravascular hemolysis. It is thus zaltenibart’s target and potentially improved safety profile (as well as infrequent dosing) that makes Novo believe its newly controlled candidate can command a meaningful portion of the $2.2 billion global PNH market. Novo will also likely pursue the rare kidney disease indication complement 3 glomerulopathy, for which Omeros was conducting a small Phase 2 study.

Other Pipeline Candidate

It should be noted that in addition to its two complement system assets, Omeros anticipates entering a Phase 1b trial for OMS527, an oral PDE7 inhibitor targeting cocaine abuse, sometime in 2026.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

Returning to the Novo out-licensing deal, the company has yet to provide details on how it expects to allocate its newfound largesse, but debt reduction might be in play. In addition to the now $80.5 million of convertible debt, it held a term loan of $87.3 million due in 2028, all against cash and investments of $28.7 million on June 30, 2025. The private placement executed in July 2025 kept Omeros barely in compliance with its credit agreement, which stipulates maintaining a minimum cash balance of $25.0 million.

Before the transaction, Omeros held $97.9 million of 5.25% convertible notes due Feb. 15, 2026. Of that total, $70.8 million was essentially rolled over into 9.50% convertible notes due June 15, 2029; $10.0 million was converted into common stock, leaving a $17.1 million balance still due in 2026.

How the company plans to use the upfront payment from Novo to rejigger its balance sheet is a bit complicated, but this is how management outlined it on its third-quarter conference call:

At closing, a portion of the proceeds will be used to fully repay all outstanding obligations under our secured credit agreement. This includes the $67.1 million outstanding under the term loan, along with an applicable prepayment premium and accrued interest.

The repayment will eliminate all liens covenants associated with the credit agreement, including the $25 million minimum liquidity covenant. In connection with the May 2025 conversion of our 2026 convertible notes, we exchanged $70.8 million in aggregate principal amount of those notes on a one-for-one basis for newly issued 2029 notes, extending the maturity to June 2029, a period more than 3 years out. Additionally, we reached agreements with 2 holders to convert $10 million of their 2026 notes to Omeros's common stock, which was completed in September 2025. Following these transactions, [indiscernible] the principal balance of our 2026 notes has been reduced from $97.9 million to $17.1 million, which becomes due in February 2026. After repayment of the $67.1 million term loan and the $17.1 million of the '26 notes, the company's only remaining debt will be $70.8 million of the 2029 notes, which again are not due until June of 2029.

The balance sheet should be more readable after the company files their 10-K for Q4. Omeros burned through $22 million in Q3 to fund all operations.

As you can see above, the company gets relatively sparse coverage from Wall Street. On average, the prognosticators expect narsoplimab to be approved before YE25, generating FY26 revenue of $117 million and FY27 revenue of $201 million.

Verdict

The Novo deal somewhat validates Omeros’ approach but leaves the cupboard rather bare except for the binary outcome that is far from a slam dunk in December 2025. That said, the zaltenibart out-license allows the company to properly commercialize narsoplimab (if approved) and puts a floor in its stock price, making the binary risk-reward asymmetrical to the upside. Although not for the risk-averse, trading at a ~$350 million premium to net cash (assuming $340 million incoming from Novo), the significant move higher that should occur upon zaltenibart’s approval makes Omeros worthy of a small, covered call trade. Options against the equity are both liquid and very lucrative, it should be noted.

data.title: Tracking Data Center Debt
data.description:
Bespoke applies a cross-asset perspective to understanding financial markets and the economy. Below is one example published last week looking at the issuance of data center-backed securitizations and how they differ from the mortgage securitizations that drove the subprime meltdown and the Global Financial Crisis.

One common way that data centers borrow is via the asset-backed securities market. 26 of these deals have been priced this year, but they’re relatively small in the grand scheme of things: just $17.5bn of face value. That's two orders of magnitude smaller than investment grade corporate bond issuance.

It’s also noteworthy that they’re relatively low leverage, with an LTV only slightly above 50 on a weighted average basis. The combination of ample current cashflows and modest leverage means this debt can absorb a lot of decline in data center demand without leading to defaults.

To be sure, risk premiums aren’t huge, but these deals all priced at spreads significantly wider than, for instance, where Oracle (ORCL) spreads sit. In other words, this securitization is much smaller and priced more conservatively than subprime MBS ever were.

To be clear, there are credit risks swirling in the data center and AI infrastructure universe. The extreme complexity of the Beignet transaction is a good example. Meta (META) is forming a joint venture with funds controlled by Blue Owl (OWL) that also receive debt funding from PIMCO funds (via the RPLDCI 6.581 05/30/49 bond which is the single largest-ever corporate bond issue).

That bond amortizes over time based on cash flows from META itself, which serves as a development partner, cross-guarantor, and tenant of the completed data center.

That allows what amounts to debt financing (with some de-risking via the JV with OWL funds) without an impact on the META parent credit rating. These sorts of complex transactions aren’t necessarily a sign of bad things to come, but they do indicate risk.

Looking at the IG bond market, we just haven’t seen the sort of sustained debt binge that is the hallmark of historical, financial system-threatening blow-ups like the subprime mortgage debacle was.

That’s evidenced by the relatively low tech share of IG credit outstanding, or the relatively modest size of data centers in securitized transactions. If the AI trade does melt away and leave an investment bust, a debt crisis looks relatively unlikely without years more extreme investment rates funded by debt.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 8 Reasons We're Definitely NOT In A Bubble
data.description:
A recent trending article from fellow analyst Rational Techne presented eight reasons why we may be in a bubble. I think the points presented are interesting and worth debating. That’s why I am writing this article, outlining 8 counter reasons why I think we may not, after all, be in a bubble.

A Story Has Captured the Collective Investor Mindset

Artificial Intelligence is, without a doubt, the trending topic in investing today. And it has been since the launch of ChatGPT. However, contrary to what many believe, this is not something that started with ChatGPT.

AI became a “catchall” word with the launch of ChatGPT. But this is a field that has decades of history, with terms like “Machine Learning” or “Neural Networks” previously used to describe it. The chart below shows how notable AI models have been evolving since the 1950s, seeing a drastic acceleration and exponential growth in the last decade.

The “problem” with framing AI, in my view, is exactly that this is a field that has been evolving exponentially. This article from 2015, for example, has been calling out how AI would evolve (exponentially), a full six years before the launch of ChatGPT to the public.

As humans, we have an inherent difficulty in framing exponential growth as a mathematical concept, as exponential growth is very rare in nature. That’s despite the fact that we have evolved, as a civilization, exponentially.

But as long as AI keeps evolving exponentially, as it has done since we started working on it, the fact that it is a hot topic in investing does not matter. Nor does it matter if we have valuations reflecting high expectations, which brings me to the next section.

Valuations Reflect High Expectations for the Future (Do they?)

Valuations are rich. Well, are they? I think this is true only for some hyped companies, such as Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), a company I have extensively covered on Seeking Alpha and that is undoubtedly richly valued. For companies like Palantir (of which I really count a few), I do believe the exponential element of growth of AI warrants high valuations (I will come back to this later).

But for the broader market, I present a different view: the idea that the stock market is not really overvalued. The problem is that we are measuring it in US dollars.

The S&P 500 measured in gold, an asset that no government can print out of thin air or spend, is roughly where it was in 2016 (see chart below).

In other words, when we measure returns in something that is not a fiat currency, we have seen a full decade of underperformance in US stocks.

What we have seen, in my view, is not a multi-decade exuberance in the market. It rather is a collapse in credibility in all fiat currencies, including the US dollar (something I covered here in detail, and I will discuss again later in this article).

“This Time is Different”

My fellow Seeking Alpha analyst argues that:

The blank slate nature of the technology allows investors to project all their hopes and dreams for the future onto it with minimal basis for pushback, again, because of the novelty.

I have two problems with this take. First, as I already outlined, AI is not really a novelty. It is a multi-decade phenomenon that only started finding its most obvious, consumer-facing application (ChatGPT) in 2021. Applications have already evolved immensely from 2021, and I expect them to keep evolving.

Below are some examples of AI applications that are nascent and accelerating, which I report from a recent article I wrote about investing in AI:

  • Self-driving vehicles, which are already being rolled out in the United States and China in the form of taxis, mostly face regulatory hurdles to mass roll-out.

  • Highly sophisticated personal assistants. My idea is that everyone will soon be able to have a human-level assistant to interact with. Not only are there already many AI assistants, but this is one of the most obvious and “easier” applications of LLMs. I think the fact that Apple’s Siri was recently integrated with ChatGPT is a sign we are on the verge of AI assistants becoming mainstream.

  • New AI-enhanced smart devices. I think the end of the smartphone will inevitably come, and AI will have a lot to do with it. For example, see Meta Platforms (META)’s AI Glasses, which the company is investing heavily in as a potential smartphone alternative.

  • AI-driven hyper-customized content. We already have increasingly realistic short videos, and the next step may be TV shows created on the spot for users, with plot pivots based on users’ reactions.

  • Personalized healthcare solutions, ranging from LLMs helping in research to improving the accuracy and speed of diagnosis, which is already considered increasingly valuable.

Speculation (Not all AI companies are winners)

A common argument I often see from AI bears is that basically all applications of AI are not turning a profit yet. I completely disagree with this take: I think the AI race so far is clearly creating AI winners and AI losers. The problem is some observers may be tempted to generalize the AI space in one way or another, either seeing the whole space as a fad or all AI players as winners.

Who are the AI winners? Let me start once again from Palantir. A company that I believe is by far the best at understanding and monetizing AI applications, as shown by the fact they are growing at an accelerating Rule of 40 of 114%, ahead of NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

I am not going to cover my full thesis for Palantir here (you can find it on my profile), but I do think the company represents an exception that warrants its high valuation.

But it’s not only Palantir. Other AI winners, in my view, are the Mag 7 companies. For example, Meta managed to re-accelerate the growth in its Advertising solutions to almost 30% in the last few quarters, thanks to AI optimizations in its recommendation algorithms.

Other winners are Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), which have both been growing their Cloud business double digits for a few quarters.

Bad News is Good News and Good News is Good News

Nothing seems able to faze the markets. Not even unorthodox economic and trade policies from the current US administration, which I find borderline nonsensical myself.

But the reason is not that we are in a bubble (whatever that may mean). The reason, in my opinion, is currency debasement. Since the US abandoned the gold standard in 1971, we have witnessed a gradual loss in credibility for the US dollar. This has only accelerated in recent years, with:

  • The launch of Quantitative Easing in the US after the GFC.

  • The use of Zero-Interest-Rate-Policy, which inflated asset prices.

  • The fiscal recklessness of US administrations for the last decade, with ballooning government debt.

  • Most recently, the politicization of the Federal Reserve, which the current administration is pressuring to cut rates.

To understand what happens to stocks when countries lose any credibility in matters of fiscal and monetary policy, look at Türkiye. The iShares MSCI Turkey ETF (TUR) reached ATH after ATH in the last 10 years, expressed in Turkish Lira (see chart below).

This happened at a time when the Turkish President fully politicized the Central Bank of the country, ran consistent long term deficits, and used the Turkish judiciary to fight political opposition, including by jailing his main political opponent.

Once again, as a thought exercise, I invite readers to try and stop thinking in USD terms altogether. When investors have no confidence in fiat currency, they flee to assets. And this is a trend that has nothing to do with AI, nor is it bound to change, in my view.

Increased Leverage (and Fear)

The increase in retail leverage can indeed be seen as a sign of a bubble, and it has been in the past. And while a risk remains (more on this later), I also want to present a different data point, which has historically indicated bottoms. That of the Fear & Greed Index, which currently stands at “Extreme Fear,” despite the S&P 500 Index (SP500) being only about 5% of ATHs.

Concentration

Market concentration in a few Tech stocks has been a recurring theme for bears. But this is a trend that is dating well before the advent of AI. I started investing when “FAANG” was the term used to describe large Tech companies. This term then evolved through the years until the most recently used “Mag 7”.

Concentration has not been a cause of market collapse so far. And, in my view, it won’t be for the foreseeable future. I see increasing concentration as a result of two factors, which I already mentioned in this article.

First, the fact that not all companies are AI winners. I think it is only natural that those companies that are showing they can monetize AI the best are also those reaping the most benefits in the stock market. AI is not an easy business. Few startups or companies outside the Tech industry can easily catch up with incumbent Tech players.

The second reason is the fact that currency debasement and a collapse in the credibility of the USD is driving up prices and valuations, even in historical terms.

High Levels of Capital Expenditures

Yes, companies are spending a lot of capital to build data centers and acquire GPUs. And they are doing so in a “circular” way, with many arguing that in itself is borderline illegal behavior.

As I outlined in a recent article, I disagree with this take. I think high CapEx is simply a symptom of an acceleration in the AI race. As long as we see more applications of AI coming and impacting the economy, this is sustainable.

And the fact we are seeing a “circular” economy in AI is simply a fast integrating supply chain in a highly sophisticated industry. Similarly to what happens in aerospace, an industry with significant barriers to entry and only two main players and five major OEMs. In aerospace, such a tightly integrated industry has been a source of criticism by IATA. Yet nobody calls aerospace a bubble.

Risks to being bullish AI

I may be wrong. I can only observe the AI space, its applications and companies and make my own conclusions. The beauty of this platform is that readers can see different opinions and decide for themselves which one they think is best.

I will not outline all possible elements that may indicate we are in fact in an AI bubble, as the best risk section to this article is simply the original article that inspired me to write this one.

I will, however, say one thing: I think we may still get an AI bubble. I think this is not it, but markets can quickly run into exuberance on the right catalysts. And this may happen especially if I am correct, and we are going to see an acceleration in AI applications and the earnings of AI companies.

Conclusion

People that think we are in a bubble, in my opinion, make two mistakes. First, they are unable to grasp the concept of exponential growth for AI. They cannot help but make comparisons with the dot-com bubble, forgetting that “past performance is no guarantee of future results” works both for bulls and bears. I see the dot-com bubble as largely irrelevant, and a possible “AI bubble” must be evaluated based on today’s observations about AI only.

The second mistake is forgetting the current scale of currency debasement (intended as all forms of monetary expansion). This is something that goes well beyond AI, and it is actually the trend that, in my view, drove most of the equity returns for the past decade and a half. Any asset that generates future cash flow or has any scarcity, measured in USD, is destined to appreciate indefinitely. Yes, corrections happen. But they have been ever quicker and ever shallower. Expecting a major reset that brings P/E ratios back to 1970s or 1980s levels is, in my view, a very dangerous approach.

We may still get an AI bubble, as markets can often run ahead of themselves. That can happen, especially if I am right and we are going to see AI accelerating in the upcoming months. But, today, I simply do not see a bubble just yet.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Lifts Slightly After Shutdown Ends
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment lifted slightly from its preliminary reading earlier in the month after the government shutdown ended but remains at its lowest level since 2022. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell 2.6 points (-4.9%) to 51.0 this month.

This reading makes the November figure the second-lowest sentiment reading of all-time, only trailing the record low of 50.0 set in June 2022. The latest reading was higher than the forecasted 50.3 and sentiment is now down 20.8 points (-29.0%) from one year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment was little changed this month with a 2.6 index point decrease from October that is within the margin of error. After the federal shutdown ended, sentiment lifted slightly from its mid-month reading. However, consumers remain frustrated about the persistence of high prices and weakening incomes. This month, current personal finances and buying conditions for durables both plunged more than 10%, whereas expectations for the future improved modestly. By the end of the month, sentiment for consumers with the largest stock holdings lost the gains seen at the preliminary reading. This group’s sentiment dropped about 2 index points from October, likely a consequence of the stock market declines seen over the past two weeks.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. The current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 39.4% below its average reading of 84.2 (arithmetic mean) and 38.6% below its geometric mean of 83.0, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 0th percentile of the 575 monthly data points in this series.

To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In November, the CECI dropped for a fourth straight month to 51.1, its lowest level in the index's history. This represents a -12.8% decrease from the previous month and a -20.0% decline from a year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 52.3.

Meanwhile, the CEI rose for the first time in five months. This represents a 1.4% increase from the previous month and a -33.7% drop from one year ago. This reading was above the forecast of 49.0.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations inched down from 4.6% last month to 4.5% this month. This marks three consecutive months of declines, but short-run inflation expectations still remain above the 3.3% seen in January. Long-run inflation expectations softened from 3.9% last month to 3.4% in November. These expectations are now modestly above the 3.2% January 2025 reading. Despite these improvements in the future trajectory of inflation, consumers continue to report that their personal finances now are weighed down by the present state of high prices.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB Business Optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Molson Coors: Market Overreaction Creates A Compelling High-Yield Value Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Molson Coors Beverage Company (TAP) is an alcoholic beverage company with popular beer brands such as Coors, Miller, Madri, Staropramen and many other global brands trading at some of their lowest levels since 2007.

Right now, I believe TAP offers a compelling buying opportunity, with solid financials that allows them to pay a nearly double-digit dividend plus buyback yield despite the weakness we see in the environment due mostly to external factors.

As we can see, Molson Coors’ free cash flow varied quite a bit over the years, with $1.24 billion in 2024, $1.41 billion in 2023 and $840.6 million in 2022, any of which would be solid for the company’s market cap and still allow a solid dividend yield, which I’ll mention below.

TAP expects to reach the lower end of their guidance, which implies a 3% to 4% decline in net sales (constant currency), 7% to 10% diluted EPS decline and a $1.3 billion (plus or minus 10%) underlying free cash flow ($710 million YTD). As I’ll mention below, this is normal in this environment, and the main reason behind it has been the weaker demand that came as a result of macro factors affecting discretionary budgets and affordability.

Since October 1, 2025, TAP has a new CEO that can potentially bringing a newer vision while also having 24 years of experience in the company, still focusing on economy brands given their proven importance in this type of environment, strengthening the core brands and continuing to expand their premiumization, or repositioning their beverages to become increasingly higher-end and better quality products, and non-alcoholic beverages in and outside the US, based on the Q3 report.

They also expect ~$650 million in CapEx, which is a cut compared to recent years that comes at a time when the company is cutting costs to adapt to this environment, very recently announcing the restructuring of their American business unit, planning to fire ~400 employees by the end of 2025, meaning a ~9% reduction. As the CEO pointed out when talking about this move during the Q3 Earnings Release, they are planning to redeploy this capital into M&As to fill portfolio gaps while also aiming to return strong yields with a prudent approach on the balance sheet:

We intend to redeploy some of these savings to step-up our investments behind key brands, commercial capabilities and in supply chain and technology that support ongoing productivity and efficiency. And we will continue to be disciplined stewards of our capital, using a dynamic capital allocation approach, balancing investments in M&A to fill portfolio gaps, while continuing to return cash to shareholders. We'll be sharing more on capital allocation in the near future. But today, let me be very clear on 2 things. First, we seek scalable deals that we expect to be accretive to both top and bottom line and are prudent from a balance sheet perspective. And second, we remain committed to our dividend and to our share repurchase program as we continue to view our stock as a compelling investment. Now there is a lot of work to do, but we see a clear path forward. Results will take some time, but we are moving with a sense of urgency.

To expand and diversify in the face of the premiumization I'll mention below, TAP went through several deals including buying a majority stake in ZOA Energy in 2024 (a better-for-you” energy drink), taking a small stake in Fever-Tree (OTCPK:FQVTF) in early 2025 (getting exclusive rights to market their cocktail mixers and tonic water in the US), acquiring a 75% equity interest in Blue Run last year while also selling four of their craft breweries to Tilray Brands, Inc. (TLRY), in a move to go beyond its traditional beer and accelerating the non-alcoholic and “better-for-you” beverage exposure.

Financially, based on TAP's latest report, we see a good position overall, with a good amount of cash despite some debt becoming current, as you can see below. The company’s equity took a hit when they announced a big goodwill impairment this year (~$3.65 billion), which is why the equity is now only slightly above the market cap. Still, the value of these assets relies mostly on the intangibles, which is not really a reliable metric since a wide majority of this value comes from brands (see page 17 here), whose value ultimately depends on what a buyer is willing to pay for them if sold, and can change significantly based on trends, popularity, image, etc.

A big chunk of Molson’s debt is due in July 2026, and a negative thing to remember is that as they’ll refinance this, it’s likely to see much higher borrowing costs compared to the 3% and 3.44% these two notes currently come with. On the bright side, after this portion of their debt is refinanced, the nearest maturity comes in 2032, so the company has ages to wait for better refinancing conditions and can potentially even reduce their more expensive debt if they want to, although they can probably find better things to do with their money.

Dividends and Buybacks

As for the shareholder returns, Molson Coors pays a ~4% dividend yield now that costs them about $372 million per year, which is a very manageable amount and a very solid payout ratio of about 33.82% based on a ~$1.1 billion free cash flow. Meanwhile, they also have a similar buyback program, with $334.9 million spent over the 9 months of 2025 compared to $437.2 million in 9M’24, getting to a combined yield of ~8.9% and a ~74.3% payout ratio.

Risks and Opportunities

What I’d argue is by far the main factor behind the bad performance seen in recent years is the weak demand due to rate hikes and the overall bad environment for the consumer. In the US for example, virtually every company that depends significantly on discretionary budgets — such as Nike, Inc. (NKE), LVMH Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton, Société Européenne (OTCPK:LVMHF), PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) and many others — have been reporting demand issues and even falling sales for years since the pandemic.

However, that also means that rate cuts will be key since they depend extensively on discretionary budgets. By increasing these rates, the Fed fights inflation by slowing down the economy, which directly impacts our discretionary budgets since variable mortgage/credit costs go up on top of the increase in prices (inflation) that often outpaced salary growth.

As a result, when these rates are cut, we can see it eventually flowing into the demand for Molson Coors’ products, while they’d also lower the company’s interest expenses as they refinance them and allow them to expand with cheaper new debt.

However, the US Government just went through its longest shutdown in history, and even though it’s over now, it came with a very important side effect for these cuts: the BLS halting its data collection and publishing during it, with a new release only in November. What it means is that we’re looking at even less up-to-date data for the Fed’s next meeting at a time when I believe we could’ve seen the effects of tariffs, on top of the uncertainty around inflation and unemployment that Powell highlighted during the October meeting.

This comes as the company is facing pressure from tariffs and a generally weak environment, continuing to expect the US beer category to fall by 4% to 6% in the second half of 2025, previously estimating $20 million to $35 million in costs in H2 due to the aluminum tariffs, as well as other indirect costs related to Trump’s tariffs to always keep in mind, such as potential cost increases in the supply chain, warehousing, transport, etc.

One of the most significant trends to keep in mind in this case is premiumization, which is why they’re diversifying more into various niches such as zero alcohol, flavored beer, energy drinks, etc., which is a trend seen in the industry since several years ago, when these companies realized that the new wave of consumers focus less and less on beer sold for the volume and put more value on the “experience” itself rather than the volume. It doesn’t mean people won’t buy six packs anymore (the equivalent of 2 liter bottles in some countries), but the average consumer may be inclined to pay a higher price for a product that feels more tailored, premium, or aligned with their lifestyle. In a way, this was always the case, but we now have significantly more options and variety.

However, don’t mistake this trend for the recent issues in the economy. This is a trend that’s been tracked since the 1950s as far as I could find, with this article from the Brewers Association from a decade ago showing the massive rise in craft, import, super-premium and FMB skyrocketing since the early 1990s, so don’t mistakenly think this is something now.

If anything, the recent environment likely slowed this trend due to the de-premiumization it came with since discretionary budgets went down at a time when prices went up, which is a trend seen in the years since the pandemic in many industries. Premium still grew while other volumes went down in basically everything since the pandemic, but I believe this growth would’ve been even more significant if we didn’t go through this economic environment.

Still, Molson’s management still has a lot of work left, as the steps they take now will be vital for their future in the face of this unstoppable trend. Especially in this industry, bad moves can be very costly, and by the time they write off these investments they can be left with an underperforming portfolio, declining cash flows and elevated debt, so there’s definitely plenty of execution risk to keep in mind.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Anheuser-Busch Anheuser-Busch InBev SA/NV (BUD), Constellation Brands, Inc. (STZ), The Boston Beer Company, Inc. (SAM), Heineken N.V. (OTCQX:HEINY), Carlsberg A/S (OTCPK:CABGY) and even Ambev S.A. (ABEV), we actually see the most attractive ratios from almost every point of view, while Molson Coors is also paying the highest dividend in the group. Given their size, we could even see a larger peer try to buy them and deliver accretive results per share since they’re still making massive amounts of money for the costs, although their potential alone might be better, this could offer a solid margin of safety though.

Valuation

To be more conservative from the start, I’ll use a $1 billion FCF in 2025, with a 4% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to rate cuts, cost savings and growth initiatives, followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll use a WACC of 8.5% (above my conservative estimate of 8.3% based on a cost of equity of 9.25% and ~6.8% cost of debt based on estimated future debt costs), $5.34 billion in net debt, $343.7 million in non-controlling interests (including redeemable) and 197.9 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $18.01 billion, an equity value of $12.32 billion and an intrinsic value of $62.27 per share based on a relatively conservative estimate, which is well above the current level.

Conclusions

I rate Molson Coords a Buy given their significant recovery potential, undervaluation and very solid yield, being ready to take advantage of long-term tailwinds while also financially healthy enough to navigate current headwinds. There is risk, of course, especially due to the environment, but the company still makes a ton of money despite the weak environment, while the overall financial position is strong and their expansion/diversification moves have potential to offset these risks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ULTY: Leveraging It For The Highest Returns
data.description:
Among investors with a primary focus on growth or income, there probably isn't a more polarizing ETF than YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (ULTY). With the recent announcement that it is going to implement a 10-for-1 reverse split, it got the usual suspects out on the growth side, using the news to attempt to undermine ULTY once again. The purpose of the fund is to generate income primarily via a covered call strategy. The key holdings at the time I'm writing are large tech stocks.

On the pro side of the ETF, it's the high-yield income generated from the covered calls that attract investors seeking consistent revenue streams, while the major con concerning ULTY is the NAV erosion it has been experiencing since its inception.

In this article, I want to talk about those two things, but more importantly, show how to leverage the income stream of the ETF for maximum effect while mitigating some of the downside risk.

Income Vs. NAV erosion

For those that take the growth side of the argument, NAV erosion is always the thing that is brought up to discourage investors from considering investing in high-yield ETFs like ULTY. In listening to or reading many of those arguments, I have found that many of those bring up NAV erosion don't even know what it really is.

For example, they think if the price of a covered call ETF drops in price, that is NAV erosion in and of itself. While it could be NAV erosion, many times it's not. NAV erosion is essentially an ETF paying out more than the income it is producing. That is what actually erodes the NAV, not the share price of an ETF moving up and down in conjunction with an underlying stock or basket of underlying stocks.

In the case of ULTY there is some actual NAV erosion, but I don't really care one way or the other about that; I'm in it for the income and not growth, as anyone taking a position in ULTY should understand for themselves.

A lot of investors really don't understand what ULTY is and why it was created, so they find themselves either being talked out of taking a position or selling off a position in response to the share price of ULTY dropping.

The bottom line is, ULTY is not a yield trap, and is doing exactly what it should be doing, understanding that will include some NAV erosion over time. As for those that suggest it is going to zero, as some like to assert, that's the same as saying all the underlying stocks above are going to zero too. It's not going to happen.

What Is ULTY?

The purpose of ULTY, as with other synthetic covered call strategies, is to take advantage of volatility in the underlying stocks in order to generate a revenue stream.

What it isn't designed to do is produce stock growth over the long term. To even use a growth or NAV erosion argument as a reason to stay away from high-yield powerhouses like ULTY is to make a mistake in my opinion.

Many arguments against ULTY are to use the S&P 500 as a benchmark to measure its performance against. Again, the purpose for the high-yield ETF is to create an income stream for the investor, and growth benchmarks don't make any sense when we understand that the very nature of covered calls is to limit the upside potential of the ETF in exchange for income.

The growth adherents think this is a bug, when in fact it's a positive feature of the holding; it's what it's built to do.

Another odd argument against ULTY has been the volatility associated with volatile stocks that include more risk. But that's the point. Again, this is what a covered call ETF is supposed to engage in, i.e., that volatility is what drives the income strategy.

I've heard far too many implications that there are hidden things associated with high-yield ETFs like ULTY, where there is a surprising amount of NAV erosion that wasn't visible to the investor. But that simply isn't true. All one has to do is read the prospectus to understand the pros and cons of taking a position in ULTY. And it couldn't be any clearer than its purpose is to produce an income stream for those taking a position in the fund.

Finally, some of the assertions made about ULTY are disingenuous because those that don't like this type of investment usually fail to mention that it's the total return of the ETF, not just how much NAV it's losing or how far the share price has fallen.

In most cases, I'm aware of, those that hold on for the long-term end up with a positive return. It's those that panic from not understanding ULTY that tend to underperform or take a big hit. Under that scenario, ULTY does what it's built to do, but not understanding that has resulted in a significant number of investors running for the exits when, if they held on with patience, would have done well over the longer term.

Leveraging For Maximum Returns

Another major thing to consider with ULTY is the allocation of capital from the income generated. This is something that really can't be tracked. I in fact have taken a significant amount of my revenue from ULTY and reinvested it in other stocks or ETFs. This isn't talked about much from detractors of high-yield ETFs.

So, not only is there the income generated from ULTY, but the deployment of capital into, hopefully, other profitable stocks or ETFs. Over time this boosts income, or if put into growth stocks, the overall value of the portfolio. The point here is, ULTY can't or shouldn't be taken as a standalone holding, but part of a strategy that is part of a growth and income portfolio.

That said, I'm aware of some unwise investors that go all in on an ETF like ULTY, by which I mean a large percentage of their portfolio is in it. In that case, there is a lot of risk associated with it, and I wouldn't recommend that as how to play it, especially if there are certain income goals you're trying to achieve.

I mention that because the distribution yield can fluctuate from week-to-week or month-to-month, and if you don't have an income cushion in place as part of your strategy (much higher income than needed to live on), than any surprise to the downside could result in some challenging financial circumstances.

Leveraging ULTY income to use it acquire other ETFs or stocks in order to have healthy and antifragile portfolios is a more desirable way to produce the desired outcome.

Conclusion

NAV erosion has little meaning to me in regard to my covered call ETF holdings. It's simply a part of using these types of investments and should be considered part of taking a position in them. I'm in it for the income, as should anyone else be that buys shares of ULTY. There is no other purpose to hold it.

Remember, the purpose of ULTY is to use volatility to generate income for the investor. Whether or not there's growth involved or not, or NAV erosion, really doesn't matter.

Over time, ULTY will probably experience decline in some of its distribution, and that's likely to happen in the current market environment in mid-November 2025. Even so, once this period of weakness is over, I expect the distributions will rebound beyond where they are today. There should also be some recovery of NAV for those concerned with such things, albeit at a modest rate.

If the NAV deteriorates to concerning levels, resulting in significant outflows, I think the response of management would be to gradually lower the distribution yield. But even in that scenario, ULTY will continue to outperform on the income side of the equation.

The recent reverse split, which will go in force in the latter part of November, will help those that used margin and leverage to build up positions in ULTY. While I think there will continue to be more NAV erosion in the near term, I believe once the tech sector returns to favor, that will be mitigated until the next correction. This will give a longer runway for those employing that type of strategy to supercharge their income.

Contrary to the naysayers, I really like ULTY and have no problem holding it for the long term. I see its distribution yield dropping some, but once economic conditions are more favorable, I believe it'll reverse direction in regard to NAV and distribution yields.

data.title: Bowhead Specialty Climbs Then Falls
data.description:
Bowhead Specialty Insurance (BOW) is a specialty lines insurance group providing casualty, professional liability and healthcare liability insurance products. Bowhead listed on the NYSE just back in May 2024. Bowhead, founded in 2020, has close ties with American Family Insurance which is a Wisconsin based mutual insurer. Bowhead originates a large proportion of its business from AmFam.

Excess and Surplus Lines

Bowhead operates in the excess and surplus lines (E&S) marketplace, which operates outside the heavy regulator environment of the mainstream admitted markets. The E&S market serves harder to place risks, which cannot be profitably underwritten using standard rates and policy forms. As such, AmFam is able to offer solutions to its policyholders which might fall outside its own risk appetite. Where AmFam is the insurer of record, the risk is reinsured 100% to Bowhead. This is known as 'fronting'.

The E&S marketplace has grown rapidly in recent years, as admitted insurance companies have been struggling to profitably underwrite more complex risks.

You can read more about the E&S segment here.

The challenge has been both in property lines, with an increased frequency of weather related losses, and in casualty lines, where the challenge has been elevated levels of claims costs. The industry has coined the phrase 'social inflation' to describe claims amounts increasing much higher than CPI or wage inflation. This is due to increased legal costs, increased litigation of claims, and larger court awards for injured parties.

This rapid growth, and the need for risk bearing capital to step in to support these more challenging risks, has stimulated significant interest from both private equity, and public markets.

Bowhead Share Performance

Bowhead had a very successful IPO, listing at $17, with a rapid share price increase to a peak of $42.29. Over the last few months however, the price has quickly fallen back to Earth at $27.61 at the time of writing.

This price drop seems to be company specific, as the insurance segment, represented by the SPDR S&P Insurance ETF (KIE) has had stable growth over the period.

Company Profile & Performance

Bowhead writes a long tail casualty based book of business, with just over $800m in premium and a combined ratio of 95.6%. This indicates that the business is generating an underwriting profit, and not relying just on investment income. The underwriting margin, however is on the low side for a company which is by design operating in the more challenging risk space.

Roughly two thirds of the business is general casualty, with the remaining third professional liability and healthcare liability. Healthcare liability covers both the hospitals and individual professionals against third party liability and malpractice claims. Professional liability cover is for other professionals, such as Lawyers, Accountants, Investment Advisors, and so forth, and the category can also include company Directors and Officers.

There is also a small specialty business.

Bearing in mind that the E&S market serves the harder to place risks, in my view this positions Bowhead at the 'pointy end' of town from a business risk profile perspective. An ROE of 13% is higher than the industry average which is expected to be around 10%.

Bowhead reported 3rd Quarter results on Nov 4th.

The top line growth was strong with gross written premiums increasing 17.5% to $231.5 million, however the latest quarter growth was at a slower rate than the year to date, which saw 25% growth.

Similarly, the net income growth for the quarter was 25%, compared to 58% for the year to date.

Both are solid prints, but the tailing rate of growth is worth keeping an eye on.

On the key metrics, the loss ratio increased from 64.5% to 65.9% while the expense ratio fell from 29.9% to 29.5%, resulting in a higher combined ratio of 95.4% compared to 94.4%.

The loss ratio deterioration resulted from a negative 1.1% in prior year reserve development. Management was at pains to paint this as a technical adjustment on the Q3 call:

This is not based on actual losses settling for more than reserved, and did not represent an increase in estimated reserves on unresolved claims.

I find this somewhat disingenuous, as an increase in the expected loss ratio for incurred but not yet reported claims indicates that management accepts that the original pricing of the earlier years was inadequate. The adjustment would be made in the light of observed loss trends in the Bowhead business and in the industry in general. This is very relevant to the profit outlook for the company.

Casualty Reserving Trends

Those that follow my work will be familiar with my concerns around casualty reserving trends.

This excellent report from actuarial consultant Willis Towers Watson explains the issues well.

Bowhead argues that, as they started the underwriting business only in 2020, they are not involved in the most difficult years, however, as shown in this chart, years 2021, 2022, and 2023 already show signs of loss underestimation. These years are still under-developed. The average claim for a book like Bowheads is probably not finalised for several years after the accident date, which leaves plenty of risk for further deterioration in prior years results.

I would not assume that this adjustment from Bowhead is a one-off, and would be looking for loss triangles from the 10-k for more information, when these are released at year end.

Valuation

On a price to earnings basis, Bowhead sits in the middle of the pack of peers that operate with large E&S exposures.

On a price to book value perspective, Kinsale sits at the low end.

Summary

Bowhead had a very successful IPO, but the share price has since returned to equivalent price to earnings valuations as peers.

The company has scaled fast post IPO, and is still posting good growth, but at a lower rate.

The company operates in the hard to place E&S market, where underwriting outperformance is key to returns. The company began underwriting in 2020, which makes evaluation of reserves for this long tail book more challenging.

The Q3 adverse prior year development is in line with trends observed in the industry, and could be a sign of further pain in the future. It will be important to watch the PYD in future quarterly reports, and especially to look for the loss triangles. In a rapidly growing portfolio of long tail lines, under-reserving can be hidden in the financial results by growing premiums, so the individual accident years need to be examined.

I would like to see more data before forming a view on reserve adequacy.

For now, I am assigning Bowhead a Hold rating.

data.title: Archrock: Politics Still Overshadows Results Unfortunately
data.description:
Archrock, Inc. (AROC) reported a good third quarter. But as the last article indicated, politics is overshadowing the good results. With cyclical stocks, the market often looks ahead. In this case, the market does not like what it sees as potential outcomes. Therefore, the good news of the quarter is overruled by future prospects. Right now, fundamentals are great. But the threat of a recession already has the industry shrinking, with even lower upstream budgets for the next fiscal year. This company insulates itself somewhat from those external threats by concentrating on the midstream business. Then again, this business is very different from the typical midstream business.

The start of a downturn often begins with the market ignoring good news. That would appear to be the case here. Now management is paying a higher dividend on better prospects. Management also believes that the dividend is defensible. That is all well and good. But with the stock price signaling a warning at this point, it would be wise for investors to pay attention until the stock begins to again respond to good news.

Earnings

Not only were earnings good, but management is currently reporting firm rates as well. That is good news for the time being.

Even the company charts (as shown above) point towards a cyclical top. Investors that choose to "buy and hold" have to realize that they will likely be holding through a downcycle. It is therefore important to not panic and sell at a likely market bottom (when the time comes).

This seems to happen more often than not. An investor sees no reason to sell now because the news is excellent, as the summary above shows. But with cyclical stocks, the stock price itself often signals a market top before it is obvious that the company news is confirming a cyclical top.

For that reason, this will continue to be a "hold" as the stock price is equally likely to decline from here as it is to appreciate. Any investor at this point has got to evaluate exactly what the situation is and what they as an investor want to see long-term. This is a place where there is no one clear answer because risk profiles vary greatly. The "hold" rating caters to both sides of the equation because right now, there is no one right answer or asymmetric return given the current conditions.

Fleet Desirability

The current fleet is in a much better position to tolerate a downturn than was the case a few years back.

One thing to realize is that the fleet continues to age during a downturn. If the downturn turns out to be such that much of the fleet becomes idle, those compressors still age even while idle. Older is less attractive to potential customers than is younger, regardless of the performance characteristics.

Also, the fleet has adjusted to the demand of larger horsepower units than was the case several years ago. All of this means that from an inventory viewpoint, the company is in good shape for a cyclical downturn.

Debt

The leverage issue that had long been a target for significant improvement appears to be finally reasonable.

With this industry, there is always a greater threat of repricing contracts when leasing rates soften. Unlike much of the midstream industry, renegotiating contracts is a bit more common here. Therefore, a conservative debt ratio when times are good is almost mandatory.

It can be seen in the past when that debt ratio climbed from a combination of returns and softer rates due to a cyclical downturn. The marketing emphasis on the midstream business will soften the impact. But as shown above, it does not prevent effects from being felt.

Overall, the balance sheet debt ratio does indicate that this company should withstand a cyclical downturn if an investor chooses to hold the stock throughout the business cycle.

Note that it would also appear to be clear that the time to consider initiating a position would be when the debt ratio is climbing. At that point, the market would likely fear more bad news ahead, and the stock price would likely reflect that. That would be when a contrarian strategy would likely work best. Now it seems to be too early for that.

Financial Trends

This company has also begun to grow an aftermarket business that should prove to be countercyclical. That business probably needs to become larger. But it is a good start at offsetting the perceived cyclical nature of the business.

The "perceived" part is the key to the stock price action. As shown above, this company has survived some really challenging market bottoms (like fiscal year 2020) in reasonably good shape. However, it is also clear that Mr. Market has other ideas.

One of the continuing challenges of this industry is maintaining enough of the proper sized compressors that are continually in demand. More than once, this company has had to upgrade its inventory to keep up with demand changes. For now, this does not appear to be an issue. But technology changes upstream have got to be constantly monitored "just in case."

Summary

Currently, the business is in good shape:

The earnings comparison alone would normally be followed with a significant stock price increase. This is especially true since management followed this good report with a bullish outlook.

But then again, the energy industry is a low visibility industry where things can change pretty quickly. Mr. Market appears to be sure that things will change quickly.

Meanwhile, management has updated guidance for the fiscal year in a positive direction. But even that has really not brought much of a stock price reaction.

Therefore, this stock continues to be a hold idea until a clear direction presents itself. Most likely the business cycle will continue downward because the Dallas Federal Reserve is already showing industry shrinkage combined with lower budgets for the next fiscal year. If this continues as Mr. Market expects, then industry conditions are likely to soften in the next fiscal year.

But that means now is the time to make a decision as to whether or not to hang onto this stock through a likely cyclical downturn that lies ahead. That, of course, depends upon the risk profile of the investor. There is no one right answer.

Now it is also possible that all the current fears disappear, and the market resumes its ever higher "winning" ways. There is an old saying on Wall Street that the market got "nine of the last six" recessions right. That saying alone tells an investor that not all recession fears happen. Right now, though, economics would appear to back the current fears. Therefore, we will have to see what actually happens.

Risks

Even though this company markets towards the midstream part of the business, there is a cyclical characteristic to the business. An extreme and sustained downturn can result in large numbers of compressors returned that remain idle until the next recovery.

On the upside of the business because future commodity prices are both volatile and low visibility, demand for midstream capacity often follows upstream once a recovery is well underway. Therefore, a recovery in this business often lags what is happening with upstream.

Anything on inventory can become obsolete with technology changes. There is not a lot of notice when that happens. Therefore, the capital budget can balloon unpredictably in the name of "keeping up" with the market demand for a new (either) size or type of compressor.

This industry does not have a lot of barriers to entry. Avoiding competition often involves a solid relationship with the customer. The aftermarket business helps here as well because this company aims to be a "one-stop shop". As a result of the competition, there is often a delay in passing on cost increases.

data.title: Napco: Strong Q1 Execution Doesn't Fully Justify The Premium Multiple
data.description:
I covered Napco Security Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ:NSSC) back in August with a fairly simple view: The recurring engine was strong enough to carry the business through a weak hardware year, and once the equipment segment stopped dragging, we would see a much cleaner earnings profile.

Q1 FY26 has delivered on that with revenue, margins, and profitability. All of them are up, but I’m more comfortable sticking with a Hold for now.

Q1 FY26 Snapshot

Metric

Q1 FY26

YoY Change

Total Revenue

$49.2M

+11.7%

Recurring Service Revenue (RSR)

$23.5M

+11.6%

Equipment Revenue

$25.7M

+11.8%

Total Gross Margin

56.6%

+70 bps

RSR Gross Margin

90.3%

Slightly lower

Equipment Gross Margin

25.7%

+210 bps

Operating Income

$13.6M

+15.1%

Net Income / EPS

$12.2M / $0.34

+8.8% EPS

Cash

$105.8M

Higher sequentially

RSR Run Rate

~$95M

Up slightly

Taking one look at the figures in the table above shows us that Napco is doing well, so the question becomes: Is it justifiable to keep a bullish position? I’m bullish on the business, less so on the price.

Why? Well, hardware isn’t a drag anymore—equipment sales grew almost 12% YoY, and I’m especially pleased that the segment’s gross margins are back above 25%. That’s not the same level as its historicals, but it’s a big step up from last year’s figure.

The company’s annual recurring service revenue (RSR) is also doing well. That’s because of the monthly RSR, which is up 11.6% YoY to $23.5 million, and it’s moved Napco’s expectations for its run rate up to $95 million. With these figures, we can see that the demand for its products, especially Starlink radios, is steadily increasing. Remember that Napco’s RSR still has gross margins >90%, and the 0.8 percentage point drop to 90.3% in Q1 is because the company is giving bulk pricing to bigger consolidators, so it gets bigger sales volumes. That’s normal, and I like it because it doesn’t affect the underlying compounders.

Leading on from that, we have profitability. I already mentioned RSR’s impressive margins, but the overall business did pretty well. Gross margin was up 70 basis points to 56.6%, operating income grew faster than revenue, and Napco’s EPS ($0.34) was a new company record for a first quarter. So, we have both of the business’s segments growing at double digits, and there is over $100 million in cash. Napco doesn’t have any debt, dividend coverage is almost guaranteed, and the company’s leverage looks great.

In my opinion, there isn’t much to criticize about how Q1 went, so I’m looking forward to what the next quarter will bring. I believe that the hardware recovery will continue in Q2, especially because of products like MVP. MVP is Napco’s “cloud-based access control platform,” and it integrates with the company’s locking hardware. Given that we’re seeing Napco trying to increase recurring service revenue, the new subscription model it is tying MVP to is a surefire way to keep more of those customer dollars within its ecosystem.

Since the MVP platform works for both enterprise and smaller businesses, I expect to see demand for it rise going into the next few quarters. However, I don’t think we’ll see any meaningful RSR contributions or lift from it until FY27 at the earliest.

It's Beyond Reasonable Levels

At today’s levels, I’d still say that Napco is near a full valuation. ~$40/share means that the stock is trading at 32x P/E TTM and ~26x EV/EBITDA TTM. Those are decent premiums over the sector medians, but in this case, let us look at the EV/Sales multiples instead. Napco currently trades at EV/Sales multiples (6.45x FWD) that have >100% premiums over its sector medians, and it’s even worse with the P/S ratios. I’m not sure that paying over the odds for this level of growth is the smart thing to do.

Yes, both revenue streams are growing at double digits, margins are up, and RSR is compounding nicely. I can certainly understand paying up for a capital-light, debt-free business with record EPS expansion, but it looks like the stock is running hot on sentiment, and it’s even dipped a bit after Q1 FY26 results came out.

The sentiment around it is still very bullish, though: Wall Street has it as a Strong Buy, and I’m sure they’re assigning a lot of value to its hardware recovery and operating leverage. Still, I prefer to sit and watch first. All things considered, I’m really pleased with how Q1 FY26 shaped up for Napco, and I’m confident in its long-term outlook. I just think that the current price is a bit too rich, and I’m not willing to assign a buy with such a high premium.
